questions.question: Explain the difference between Comparable and Comparator interfaces in Java. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Comparable provides natural ordering, implemented by the class itself
Comparator provides custom/external ordering, doesn't modify the original class
Use Comparable when you control the source code and want single natural order
Use Comparator when using third-party classes or need multiple sort orders
Comparator is a functional interface allowing lambda usage

questions.question: What is the contract for the compare() method? Explain the return value conventions.
keyPoints: Returns negative if o1 < o2
Returns zero if o1 == o2
Returns positive if o1 > o2
Must be consistent: sgn(compare(x, y)) == -sgn(compare(y, x))
Must be transitive: compare(x,y)>0 && compare(y,z)>0 implies compare(x,z)>0
compare(x,x) must return 0

questions.question: How do you handle null values in sorting using Comparator? Explain with example.
keyPoints: Direct comparison throws NullPointerException
Use nullsFirst() to place null at beginning (null < non-null)
Use nullsLast() to place null at end (null > non-null)
Chain with naturalOrder() or custom comparator: Comparator.nullsLast(Comparator.naturalOrder())
Example: list.sort(Comparator.nullsFirst(Comparator.naturalOrder()))

questions.question: Write Java code to sort a list of Employee objects by salary (descending) and then by name (ascending).
keyPoints: Use Comparator.comparing with reversed() for descending
Use thenComparing() to add secondary sort
Code: Comparator.comparing(Employee::getSalary).reversed().thenComparing(Employee::getName)
Alternatively: Comparator.comparing(Employee::getSalary, Comparator.reverseOrder()).thenComparing(Employee::getName)
Apply using Collections.sort(list, comparator) or list.sort(comparator)

questions.question: What are the advantages of using Comparator comparingInt(), comparingLong(), comparingDouble() over comparing()?
keyPoints: Avoids autoboxing/unboxing overhead for primitive types
comparingInt() extracts int, compares as primitives
Less memory allocation (no Integer/Long/Double wrapper objects)
Better performance for large datasets with primitive fields
Method names match primitive type for clarity

questions.question: Explain method chaining in Comparator using thenComparing(). How does it work internally?
keyPoints: thenComparing() creates a composite comparator
First comparator is used, if returns 0 (equal), second comparator decides
Allows multi-level sorting: primary, secondary, tertiary, etc.
Returns new Comparator without modifying original
Internally uses short-circuit evaluation - stops at first non-zero result

questions.question: Why should you avoid using (a, b) -> a - b for Integer comparison? What is the correct approach?
keyPoints: a - b can cause integer overflow when values are near Integer.MAX_VALUE
Example: Integer.MAX_VALUE - (-1) overflows to negative
Use Comparator.comparingInt(Integer::intValue) or (a, b) -> Integer.compare(a, b)
Integer.compare() handles overflow correctly
comparingInt() also optimizes for primitive int to avoid autoboxing

questions.question: Create a complete Java program that demonstrates sorting a list of Student objects with multiple criteria: first by branch (CS, IT, ECE ascending), then by CGPA (descending), then by name (case-insensitive ascending). Include proper null handling.
keyPoints: Create Student class with branch, cgpa, name fields
Define branch order: CS < IT < ECE using Comparator with Map
Use Comparator.comparing with custom mapping for branch order
Use reversed() for descending CGPA
Use comparing for case-insensitive name (String::toLowerCase)
Chain all with thenComparing: branchComp.thenComparing(cgpaComp).thenComparing(nameComp)
Handle nulls using nullsFirst/nullsLast as appropriate
Show output demonstrating all three sort levels

questions.question: Write a Java program that uses Comparator with Lambda expressions to sort various collections: (a) String array by length, (b) List of Integer in reverse order, (c) List of custom objects by multiple fields using method reference.
keyPoints: For (a): Comparator.comparingInt(String::length)
For (b): Collections.sort(list, Collections.reverseOrder()) or list.sort(Comparator.reverseOrder())
For (c): Create Employee class with department, salary, name
Use Comparator.comparing(Employee::getDept).thenComparing(Employee::getSalary).thenComparing(Employee::getName)
Demonstrate lambda: (a, b) -> a.length() - b.length()
Show usage with Arrays.sort(), Collections.sort(), List.sort()
Include explanation of why lambdas work (Comparator is functional interface)

questions.question: Design a Comparator that can sort a list of transactions by date (newest first), and for same dates, by amount (highest first), and handle null dates gracefully by placing them at the end.
keyPoints: Create Transaction class with LocalDate date and BigDecimal amount
Handle null dates using nullsLast() as base
For non-null dates: Comparator.comparing(Transaction::getDate, Comparator.reverseOrder())
For amount: Comparator.comparing(Transaction::getAmount, Comparator.reverseOrder())
Chain: Comparator.nullsLast(Comparator.comparing(Transaction::getDate).reversed()).thenComparing(Transaction::getAmount, Comparator.reverseOrder())
Or: Comparator.nullsLast(Comparator.comparing(Transaction::getDate).reversed()).thenComparing(Comparator.comparing(Transaction::getAmount).reversed())
Show test cases with null dates, same dates, different amounts
